rawFindings.title: Missing Content-Security-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: No CSP header found. This makes the application more susceptible to XSS attacks.
rawFindings.evidence: Header "content-security-policy" not present in response

rawFindings.title: Missing X-Frame-Options Header
rawFindings.description: The X-Frame-Options header is missing, potentially allowing clickjacking attacks. Note: Target uses Vercel which may inject this header at the platform level.
rawFindings.evidence: Header "x-frame-options" not present in response

rawFindings.title: Missing X-Content-Type-Options Header
rawFindings.description: Missing X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header. Browsers may MIME-sniff responses. Note: Target uses Vercel which may inject this header at the platform level.
rawFindings.evidence: Header "x-content-type-options" not present in response

rawFindings.title: Missing Referrer-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: No Referrer-Policy set. Sensitive data in URLs may leak via the Referer header. Note: Target uses Vercel which may inject this header at the platform level.
rawFindings.evidence: Header "referrer-policy" not present in response

rawFindings.title: Missing Permissions-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: No Permissions-Policy header. Browser features like camera/microphone are not explicitly restricted.
rawFindings.evidence: Header "permissions-policy" not present in response

rawFindings.title: Missing Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: The Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header is missing. This header isolates the browsing context, preventing cross-origin attacks like Spectre.
rawFindings.evidence: Header "cross-origin-opener-policy" not present in response

rawFindings.title: Missing Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: The Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header is missing. This header ensures all cross-origin resources are loaded with CORS or CORP, enabling cross-origin isolation.
rawFindings.evidence: Header "cross-origin-embedder-policy" not present in response

rawFindings.title: Missing Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: The Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header is missing. This header prevents other origins from loading this resource, mitigating side-channel attacks.
rawFindings.evidence: Header "cross-origin-resource-policy" not present in response

rawFindings.title: Cookie "neon_consent" Missing HttpOnly Flag
rawFindings.description: The cookie "neon_consent" is accessible via JavaScript, increasing XSS impact.
rawFindings.evidence: Cookie: neon_consent; HttpOnly=false

rawFindings.title: Cookie "neon_consent" Missing Secure Flag
rawFindings.description: The cookie "neon_consent" can be transmitted over unencrypted connections.
rawFindings.evidence: Cookie: neon_consent; Secure=false

rawFindings.title: 1 Third-Party Cookies Missing HttpOnly
rawFindings.description: 1 analytics/marketing cookies are accessible via JavaScript. These are third-party tracking cookies with limited security impact.
rawFindings.evidence: Cookies: ajs_anonymous_id

rawFindings.title: 1 Third-Party Cookies Missing Secure Flag
rawFindings.description: 1 analytics/marketing cookies can be transmitted over unencrypted connections. These are third-party tracking cookies with limited security impact.
rawFindings.evidence: Cookies: ajs_anonymous_id

rawFindings.title: Missing HSTS Header
rawFindings.description: The Strict-Transport-Security header is missing. This allows downgrade attacks and cookie hijacking.
rawFindings.evidence: Header "strict-transport-security" not present in response

rawFindings.title: HTTP TRACE Method Enabled
rawFindings.description: The HTTP TRACE method is enabled on the server. This can be exploited for Cross-Site Tracing (XST) attacks to steal credentials.
rawFindings.evidence: Template trace-method-enabled matched: HTTP TRACE Method Enabled

rawFindings.title: Missing Subresource Integrity (SRI)
rawFindings.description: Found 24 external resource(s) without integrity attributes on https://neon.tech/unify. Without SRI, if the CDN or external host is compromised, an attacker can inject malicious code that the browser will execute without verification. Add integrity="sha384-..." and crossorigin="anonymous" attributes to all external script and stylesheet tags.
rawFindings.evidence:
External resources without SRI:

rawFindings.title: Server-Side Prototype Pollution via Query Parameter (__proto__-bracket)
rawFindings.description: The application is vulnerable to prototype pollution via the "__proto__[secbot_pp]" query parameter. The server-side query string parser (e.g., qs, query-string) or deep merge function (e.g., lodash.merge) allows injection of properties onto Object.prototype. This can lead to denial of service, authentication bypass, or remote code execution depending on the application logic.
rawFindings.evidence:
Technique: __proto__-bracket
Payload: __proto__[secbot_pp]=polluted
Detection: Canary value "secbot_pp":"polluted" reflected in response body
Response status: 403

interpretedFindings.title: SQL Injection in URL Parameters
interpretedFindings.description: SQL error messages are returned when injecting payloads into the 'a' and 'n' query parameters on the /unify endpoint. The evidence shows PostgreSQL-related content in error responses, suggesting user input may be interpolated into database queries without proper sanitization. Note: the 'SQL error' evidence appears to be a PostgreSQL documentation link rather than a true database error, which warrants manual verification.
interpretedFindings.impact: If confirmed, an attacker could read, modify, or delete database contents, bypass authentication, and potentially execute OS commands via the database engine. This would expose all customer data stored in the PostgreSQL backend.
reproductionSteps: Navigate to https://neon.tech/unify?a=%27+OR+%271%27%3D%271&n=pricing
Observe the response for SQL error messages or behavioral differences
Repeat with parameter n: https://neon.tech/unify?a=51b7d4a6-96d4-40e0-9ab7-4a1c076825d2&n=%27+OR+%271%27%3D%271
Use sqlmap to confirm: sqlmap -u 'https://neon.tech/unify?a=test&n=pricing' --dbms=postgresql
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Use parameterized queries / prepared statements exclusively. Never interpolate user input into SQL strings. In Node.js with pg, use $1 placeholders. Also suppress detailed error messages in production responses.

interpretedFindings.title: Directory Traversal on /unify Endpoint
interpretedFindings.description: The scanner reports that a path traversal payload (..\..\..\etc\passwd) appended to the URL returns system file content. This would allow an attacker to read arbitrary files from the server filesystem. Manual verification is recommended as Cloudflare/Vercel edge infrastructure may normalize such paths, and the response may be a false positive.
interpretedFindings.impact: If confirmed, an attacker could read sensitive server files including environment variables, private keys, application source code, and system credentials, leading to full server compromise.
reproductionSteps: Request: GET https://neon.tech/..%2F..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd
Also try: GET https://neon.tech/..\..\..\etc\passwd
Check if response contains root: or similar /etc/passwd content
Try reading .env or next.config.js: GET https://neon.tech/..%2F..%2F.env
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: In Next.js, ensure all file-serving routes validate and sanitize path inputs. Use path.resolve() and verify the resolved path starts with the expected base directory. Apply allowlists for accessible paths.

interpretedFindings.title: Server-Side Prototype Pollution via Query Parameter
interpretedFindings.description: The application reflects a canary value injected via __proto__[secbot_pp]=polluted in the response body, suggesting the server-side query string parser (likely 'qs') or a deep merge function is polluting Object.prototype. The server returned a 403, which may indicate WAF blocking, but the canary reflection suggests partial processing occurred before the block.
interpretedFindings.impact: Prototype pollution can lead to remote code execution, authentication bypass, denial of service, or privilege escalation depending on how the polluted properties are consumed downstream in the application.
reproductionSteps: Send GET https://neon.tech/unify?__proto__[secbot_pp]=polluted&a=51b7d4a6-96d4-40e0-9ab7-4a1c076825d2&n=pricing
Check if 'secbot_pp' or 'polluted' appears in the response body
Try constructor[prototype][secbot_pp]=polluted as an alternative vector
Test for RCE gadgets: __proto__[outputFunctionName]=x;process.mainModule.require('child_process').execSync('id')
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Upgrade or replace vulnerable query string parsers. Use Object.freeze(Object.prototype) at application startup. Sanitize keys in any deep merge operations. Use Map instead of plain objects for untrusted data.

interpretedFindings.title: Missing HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) Header
interpretedFindings.description: The Strict-Transport-Security header is absent from responses on neon.com. Without HSTS, browsers may connect over HTTP before being redirected to HTTPS, creating a window for SSL stripping attacks and cookie interception.
interpretedFindings.impact: Attackers on the same network (e.g., public Wi-Fi) can perform SSL stripping attacks, intercepting session cookies and sensitive data before the HTTPS redirect occurs. This is especially impactful given the missing Secure flag on cookies.
reproductionSteps: curl -I https://neon.com/?chatId=1
Verify absence of 'strict-transport-security' header in response
Use SSLStrip or similar tool on a shared network to demonstrate downgrade
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Add the HSTS header with a long max-age and includeSubDomains. In Next.js, configure this in next.config.js headers or at the Vercel/Cloudflare edge level.

interpretedFindings.title: Open Redirect on Login Endpoint via Multiple Parameters
interpretedFindings.description: The /login endpoint accepts redirect destination via at least 7 different query parameters (url, redirect, next, return, returnTo, redirect_uri, goto, dest) and reflects them in the Location header without validating that the destination is within the neon.tech domain. Note: the evidence shows the redirect goes to neon.com/login?param=... rather than directly to evil.example.com, which may indicate the redirect is not fully open — manual verification is needed.
interpretedFindings.impact: Attackers can craft phishing URLs that appear legitimate (https://neon.tech/login?next=https://evil.example.com) to steal credentials or OAuth tokens. The redirect_uri parameter is particularly dangerous in OAuth flows.
reproductionSteps: Visit https://neon.tech/login?next=https://evil.example.com
Observe if the browser is redirected to evil.example.com after login
Test redirect_uri specifically: https://neon.tech/login?redirect_uri=https://attacker.com/callback
Check if the final redirect destination is the attacker domain or stays within neon.com
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Validate all redirect parameters against an allowlist of trusted domains/paths. Prefer relative paths only. Reject any value containing '://' or starting with '//'.

interpretedFindings.title: Verbose Error — Potential Information Disclosure on /undefined Route
interpretedFindings.description: Accessing https://neon.tech/undefined returns a response containing what appears to be serialized Next.js page data including internal routing structures and content references. While the matched pattern references a PostgreSQL tutorial link (not a true DB error), the exposure of internal JSON structure and undefined route handling may reveal application internals.
interpretedFindings.impact: Leaking internal application structure, routing logic, and content organization could assist attackers in mapping the application and identifying further attack surfaces. The /undefined path suggests unhandled null/undefined values being used as route parameters.
reproductionSteps: GET https://neon.tech/undefined
Observe the response body for JSON structures, internal paths, or application metadata
Check if the response differs from a standard 404 page
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Implement a proper 404 handler in Next.js. Ensure that null/undefined values are never used as route parameters. Sanitize error responses to not expose internal data structures in production.

interpretedFindings.title: Cookie "neon_consent" Missing Secure and HttpOnly Flags
interpretedFindings.description: The neon_consent cookie is set without the Secure flag (allowing transmission over HTTP) and without the HttpOnly flag (making it accessible to JavaScript). While this is a consent/preference cookie rather than a session token, missing flags represent a defense-in-depth gap.
interpretedFindings.impact: Without HttpOnly, any XSS vulnerability (present or future) can read this cookie. Without Secure, the cookie can be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections, potentially exposing it to network interception. The impact is lower since this is a consent cookie, not a session token.
reproductionSteps: Visit https://neon.com/ and inspect cookies in browser DevTools
Confirm neon_consent cookie lacks HttpOnly and Secure attributes
Verify document.cookie in browser console returns the neon_consent value
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Set both Secure and HttpOnly flags on all cookies. For consent cookies that genuinely need JavaScript access, at minimum add the Secure flag. Use SameSite=Lax or Strict as well.

interpretedFindings.title: Missing Subresource Integrity (SRI) on External Scripts
interpretedFindings.owaspCategory: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
interpretedFindings.description: 24 external JavaScript resources on /unify are loaded without integrity attributes. These scripts are served from neon.com CDN (Next.js static chunks). If the CDN or origin is compromised, malicious code could be injected and executed in users' browsers without detection.
interpretedFindings.impact: A supply chain attack or CDN compromise could allow an attacker to inject malicious JavaScript that executes in all users' browsers, potentially stealing credentials, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of users.
reproductionSteps: View source of https://neon.tech/unify
Identify <script src="https://neon.com/_next/static/..." > tags without integrity attributes
Confirm no integrity="sha384-..." attribute is present
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: For Next.js static chunks served from your own CDN/domain, SRI is less critical than for truly third-party scripts. However, implement a strong Content Security Policy (CSP) as the primary defense. For any genuinely third-party scripts, add SRI hashes.

topIssues: SQL Injection and Directory Traversal on /unify endpoint — manually verify and remediate immediately with parameterized queries and path validation
Server-Side Prototype Pollution — freeze Object.prototype and audit query string parsing libraries for unsafe deep merge patterns
Missing HSTS header — add Strict-Transport-Security with preload to prevent SSL stripping attacks, especially given cookie flag weaknesses
